pattern: Precompute a 2-D apple suffix-sum so any rectangular region's apple count is O(1), then apply 3-D DP: dp[cuts][row][col] = number of valid ways to split the remaining sub-pizza starting at (row, col) into exactly 'cuts' pieces.
hints: Build a 2-D suffix-sum table once so you can query 'how many apples are in rectangle (r1,c1)..(r2,c2)' in O(1); you will need this for every candidate cut.

Define dp[t][r][c] as the number of ways to divide the sub-pizza whose top-left corner is (r,c) into exactly t apple-containing pieces; a base case exists when t=1 and the remaining rectangle holds at least one apple.

For each state, try every horizontal cut (giving the top strip away if it has apples) and every vertical cut (giving the left strip away if it has apples), accumulating the counts from the resulting sub-problems modulo 10^9+7.

edge_inputs.description: single piece requested, whole pizza has one apple - exactly one way
single piece requested, apple in bottom-right corner only
k=2, one apple per half - only one horizontal cut works
apple only in top-left corner, k=2, only cuts that keep that apple alone are valid
cutting off an empty strip must be forbidden - all apples are in one row
apples scattered so that some candidate cuts produce an apple-free piece and must be skipped
1x1 pizza with a single apple and k=1
k equals number of rows, forcing one horizontal cut per step in a single-column pizza
k=10 (maximum) on a grid dense with apples - large count exercising modular arithmetic

stress.description: random pizza grids up to 50x50 with k up to 10, cells independently 'A' or '.' with roughly 40% apple density
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    rows = rng.randint(1, size)
    cols = rng.randint(1, size)
    k = rng.randint(1, 10)
    pizza = [''.join('A' if rng.random() < 0.4 else '.' for _ in range(cols)) for _ in range(rows)]
    return [pizza, k]

complexity.time: O(k * rows^2 * cols + k * rows * cols^2)
